title: What does the term 'fail-fast' mean in the context of the Java Collections Framework?

answers.junior: In the Java Collections Framework, "fail-fast" refers to the behavior of an iterator that immediately throws a ConcurrentModificationException if the underlying collection is structurally modified after the iterator was created — except through the iterator's own remove() or add() methods. This detection works via an internal modCount field tracked on the collection; when the iterator is created it captures the current modCount, and on each call to next() or remove() it checks whether the field has changed, throwing ConcurrentModificationException if it has.
answers.middle: In the Java Collections Framework, "fail-fast" refers to the behavior of an iterator that immediately throws a ConcurrentModificationException if the underlying collection is structurally modified after the iterator was created — except through the iterator's own remove() or add() methods. This detection works via an internal modCount field tracked on the collection; when the iterator is created it captures the current modCount, and on each call to next() or remove() it checks whether the field has changed, throwing ConcurrentModificationException if it has. For example: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("a","b","c")); Iterator<String> it = list.iterator(); list.add("d"); it.next(); // throws ConcurrentModificationException. Most standard collections in java.util — including ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet, and LinkedList — provide fail-fast iterators. The purpose is to surface bugs early: if your code accidentally modifies a collection while iterating it, you get an immediate, loud failure rather than silent data corruption or unpredictable behavior.
answers.senior: In the Java Collections Framework, "fail-fast" refers to the behavior of an iterator that immediately throws a ConcurrentModificationException if the underlying collection is structurally modified after the iterator was created — except through the iterator's own remove() or add() methods. This detection works via an internal modCount field tracked on the collection; when the iterator is created it captures the current modCount, and on each call to next() or remove() it checks whether the field has changed, throwing ConcurrentModificationException if it has. For example: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("a","b","c")); Iterator<String> it = list.iterator(); list.add("d"); it.next(); // throws ConcurrentModificationException. Most standard collections in java.util — including ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet, and LinkedList — provide fail-fast iterators. The purpose is to surface bugs early: if your code accidentally modifies a collection while iterating it, you get an immediate, loud failure rather than silent data corruption or unpredictable behavior. It is important to understand that this mechanism is best-effort and not guaranteed — the Java documentation explicitly states it should not be relied upon for correctness in concurrent programs. For thread-safe, non-throwing behavior in concurrent code, prefer the collections in java.util.concurrent, such as CopyOnWriteArrayList and ConcurrentHashMap, whose iterators are "fail-safe" — they operate on a snapshot or use lock-free mechanisms and never throw ConcurrentModificationException. A common pitfall is trying to remove elements during a for-each loop (which uses an iterator internally) using collection.remove() instead of iterator.remove(); always use the iterator's own remove() when modifying during iteration to avoid triggering this exception.

Most standard collections in java.util — including ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet, and LinkedList — provide fail-fast iterators.
